food_subgroup: Steamed milk custard / egg-white milk pudding

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for taro double-layer milk custard. FOODON_03301710 (milk pudding food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (milk-based dessert) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for taro double-layer steamed milk custard in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dessert preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g (one bowl), consistent with typical restaurant portions of double-layer steamed milk custard served in Chinese dessert shops.

nutrient_source.title: Shuang Pi Nai (Double-Skin Milk Custard) — estimated from component ingredients: whole milk, egg white, taro, sugar; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for whole milk (FDC ID 746782), egg white (FDC ID 747997), and taro (FDC ID 170071)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for taro double-layer milk custard exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted recipe model: approximately 55% whole milk, 20% taro (cooked), 15% egg white, and 10% sugar by weight, per 100 g of finished product. USDA FoodData Central component entries used as primary evidence. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: Taro — glycemic index estimated from taro (cooked) GI value of 56 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283) and whole milk GI of 31 (University of Sydney GI Database); weighted blend for custard matrix yields estimated GI of approximately 53.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for taro double-layer milk custard. GI estimated as a weighted blend: taro (cooked) GI 56 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) at ~20% ingredient weight contribution, whole milk GI 31 (University of Sydney GI Database) at ~55% contribution, egg white GI ~0 (negligible glycemic contribution), sugar GI 65 at ~10% contribution. Weighted estimate yields approximately 53, classified as low GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (53 × 16.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 17.5, rounded to 17.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~52 mg/100 g); potassium-rich taro component may support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 104 mg sodium per standard 200 g serving, this dessert is relatively low in sodium and can be consumed occasionally by individuals with hypertension, though added sugar content should be monitored as part of overall dietary management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~53) and significant sugar content (~10.8 g/100 g); 200 g serving delivers approximately 21.6 g sugar and a glycemic load of ~17.6
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl (approximately 100 g) and consume infrequently, as the combined sugar and moderate glycemic load may cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise despite the low-to-moderate GI classification.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Relatively low saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) and low cholesterol (~12 mg/100 g); milk-based dessert with moderate energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dessert has a modest saturated fat and cholesterol profile consistent with occasional inclusion in a heart-healthy diet, but the added sugar content warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk through dietary glycaemic control.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taro Double-Layer Milk Custard (芋头双皮奶, Yùtou Shuāngpí Nǎi) is a classic Cantonese steamed milk dessert elevated with the addition of Lipu taro from Guangxi, China. The dish is a variation of the traditional Shunde double-skin milk custard (双皮奶), in which fresh whole milk is heated, cooled to form a skin, then combined with beaten egg whites and sugar before being gently steamed a second time to produce a silky, layered custard with a characteristic second skin on top. The taro component — made from freshly steamed Guangxi Lipu taro, prized for its dense, fragrant, and slightly grainy texture — is incorporated as a thick purée or layered element that adds a rich purple-grey hue, an earthy-sweet aroma, and a glutinous mouthfeel. The finished dessert is smooth and creamy at the milk custard layer, with the taro providing textural contrast and a distinctive starchy sweetness. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 224 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 7.0 g fat, and 33 g carbohydrate, with meaningful calcium (~236 mg) from the milk base. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 53 (low to moderate), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 17.6 per serving. Clinically, the dessert is moderate concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its sugar content and glycemic load, and low concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease given its modest sodium and saturated fat levels. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Commonly served warm in Chinese dessert shops (糖水铺) in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Guangdong.
common_user_queries: What is taro double-layer milk custard?
How is shuang pi nai made with taro?
Calories in taro double skin milk pudding
Is taro custard suitable for diabetics?
Taro milk custard GI glycemic index
Is double-layer milk custard gluten free?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for taro double-layer milk custard was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central entries for whole milk, egg white, taro (cooked), and sugar. GI is an analogous estimate derived from approved GI sources. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted recipe model (approximately 55% whole milk, 20% cooked taro, 15% egg white, 10% sugar by weight) using USDA FoodData Central component data. Actual restaurant preparation at RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 may differ in milk-to-taro ratio, sugar level, and serving size. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as whole milk contains only trace naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) below the threshold of nutritional significance; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central whole milk entry. 3. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2) are estimated from USDA milk and taro component fatty acid profiles. 5. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Beverages'; this has been corrected to 'Desserts' as taro double-layer milk custard is a steamed custard dessert, not a beverage. 6. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been populated from USDA component estimates. 7. Vitamin D (0.5 mcg/100 g) is estimated from the whole milk component contribution; this is an approximation as fortification levels vary by milk brand.
